# Pro Q&A: Judy Faulkner

Judy Faulkner shared her thoughts in response to the overall emphasis on opening EHR data for IT developers. | AP Photo

By [Arthur Allen](https://www.politico.com/staff/arthur-allen) 02/14/2019 07:17 AM EST

* [](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://politi.co/2SIX3Yf)
* [](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://politi.co/2SIX3Yf&text=Pro Q&A: Judy Faulkner&via=politico)
* 
*

We interviewed Epic Systems founder and CEO Judy Faulkner the day after CMS and ONC released their rules defining the demands on providers, health plans and EHR vendors for information sharing. Like most of the health IT world, Faulkner hadn’t digested the rules, but she shared thoughts in response to the overall emphasis on opening EHR data for IT developers. The interview was lightly edited for space.

**What do you think of the interoperability rules?**

I haven’t read them all but they are important. What I don’t know is: How much do we as a vendor have the responsibility to make sure things we are doing are right for the patient and the health system? For example, Cambridge Analytica got permission from users, but Facebook was still seen as being responsible and having to audit what they were doing. Health IT info is even more sensitive. Is data responsibility gone now? Are we and the health systems both to give patient data to anybody, no matter what they do with it? There are two kinds of anybodies; one is the patient, that seems reasonable. But if it’s also releasing patient data to a vendor, what responsibility does the health system have? Maybe none. What responsibility do we have? Maybe none,

**I’ve heard that some providers are worrying about the rules in terms of liability. Do you think about it in terms of liability, or responsibility?**

Yes. It’s multifaceted. [With the App Orchard and other interactions with developers], we’ve been trying to evaluate whether organizations are ethical, whether their apps are well-behaved and don’t release private health information inappropriately or bring the computer system to a halt. If there’s a breach, do you tell people? We were going down that path to make sure we were careful. There are two things — what do the rules say, and what are the right things to do?

[If app developers] are sending data into the EHR, does it become vendors’ responsibility to be sure it doesn’t hurt anything? Is it the health system’s responsibility? If they put information back and it’s inaccurate or gave it to the wrong patient or it says cut off your left arm and you shouldn’t? Or it could be something that messes up whole database. Where does that responsibility lie? Those are real and reasons why vendors have to be careful when they write code.

What I don’t know is, how much is this their meeting venture capital requests to get into the health IT area? And how much is about curing the patient? I don’t know. None of us want to do harmful things. That makes us go slower in our development because you can so easily do harmful things.

I think that the health systems and the EHR vendors and government owe the patients to protect their data. The typical patient might say “yes” to something they don’t understand. Not because they are not intelligent. You see a 55-page terms of agreement, check here. Everyone says “I agree.” No one reads them. What responsibility if any do we have? There are a lot of philosophical questions to be answered.

**Personally I don’t care about my health data, I want someone else to take care of me if I’m sick.**

I would like knowledgeable people to help guide me in what I’m supposed to do. I don’t want to be the one responsible for doing that. So yes, we have responsibilities. Or we are misleading people who think we have shouldered responsibilities that we have not?

**Don Rucker this morning said that once information gets out of the system into the hands of the patient, it’s their responsibility.**

I’m not worried about patient data getting into the hands of the patient. I’m worried about it getting into the hands of another vendor before it gets to the patient. I agree that once the data gets out of the HIPAA-protected space into the patient’s hands, it’s their responsibility. But … if we give information to patients through a vendor, then I’m worried it’s a different situation. In a sense, we’re authorizing that vendor. Unless the regulations say that’s OK to do, then OK, we do it. … I’m not going to make a philosophical judgment. I’m going to just say we’ve got to do what ever it is we’re supposed to do. But hopefully it will be reasonable.

**What did you think of the news that CNBC’s Jim Cramer was saying Apple should buy you?**

I was amazed. First of all, you have to realize, I don’t know who Jim Cramer is. I thought it was funny. It’s kind of nice he knows who we are, whoever he is. Not a single customer was concerned about it. Every single one of them knew we wouldn’t sell.

[](https://www.politico.com/story/2018/09/21/fema-brock-long-vehicles-reimbursement-ig-inspector-general-report-836410)

[FEMA chief to reimburse government for personal use of federal vehicles](https://www.politico.com/story/2018/09/21/fema-brock-long-vehicles-reimbursement-ig-inspector-general-report-836410)

By [Daniel Lippman](https://www.politico.com/staff/daniel-lippman) | September 21, 2018 10:06 PM

**What do you focus on at Epic these days?**

My number one focus is the happiness of our customers. KLAS found three things were most important for Epic customers: personalization for physicians—every physician wants to make their workflow match the software. Second, initial training. If the physician says the initial training provided by their organization was horrendous, 10 years later they’ll still say it. You need to get it right the first time and if you don’t, go back and try to get it right. Third, when the physician goes to a technical person who can make that change, how many committees does it go through? The best answer is zero.

**Do you take physician burnout personally?**

One KLAS study on physician burnout and the EHR looked at tens of thousands of physicians and found little correlation between burnout and EHRs. They found a lot who liked both their profession and their EHR. Quite a few liked their EHR and not their profession. Quite a few liked their profession and not their EHR. Very few disliked both. Quite a few of our customers say the EHR is not the key factor in burnout. We want doctors to love it. We want them to feel good, to help them take better care of patients.

**One element of the ONC rule is to require EHR companies to release screenshots or other information that might help detect potential patient harm. A lot of people have complained that Epic is too stingy with that data — and we heard that again after we described an AMA and Medstar campaign over the issue.**

When I heard about that story, I brought in the folks who evaluate those requests. I agree with the concerns of people who say we are being too stingy. I said, “If it’s a customer and it’s a reasonable number of screen shots and they are going to use them for a paper or presentation or analysis, it should immediately be a yes. Let them use the whole screen.” My feeling is we were being more restrictive than we should have. Of course it’s fair not to have our entire thousands of screens made public.

Filed Under:

* Data
* Health
* eHealth
* Epic Systems
